summary: This article presents a conceptual framework for understanding and managing the 'human gates' that slow down AI-driven automation in commercial pipelines. It categorizes these gates and outlines practical responses for teams. The work offers a useful perspective on operationalizing AI in business contexts.
strengths: Identifies and categorizes critical human-authority gates in AI automation workflows.
Provides a clear conceptual framework for understanding bottlenecks in AI-assisted delivery.
Offers practical suggestions for managing these human-dependent steps.
weaknesses: Lacks empirical data or case studies to substantiate the claims about "dominating wall-clock time" or specific failure patterns.
The novelty is moderate, as the concepts of human oversight and operational bottlenecks in software delivery are not entirely new, though the framing around AI agents is timely.
No supporting repository or code is provided, limiting reproducibility and direct verification of the proposed practical responses.
suggestions: Include a case study or a more detailed example to illustrate the described failure patterns and practical responses.
Quantify the impact of these 'human gates' if possible, perhaps through a survey or by referencing industry reports.
Consider expanding on the 'Scope' section to discuss how this framework might evolve as AI capabilities advance.

claims.text: Large language model agents can propose patches, tests, and refactors faster than a typical human reviewer can read them.
claims.source: General knowledge about LLM capabilities and software development workflows.

claims.text: Monetization still crosses boundaries that require human authority—OAuth consent, payment provider dashboards, DNS registrars, and secret rotation.
When these steps are treated as an afterthought, swarms produce code that cannot be exercised in production, so revenue experiments never receive real traffic.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Singleton Control Planes and Mutex Discipline in Agent Swarms Using Shared Human Interfaces
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission focuses on the 'human gates' as a specific type of bottleneck, whereas the existing paper discusses broader control plane and mutex discipline in agent swarms.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Canonical Task Systems Versus Ephemeral Coordination in Multi-Agent Swarms
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission provides a conceptual categorization of human-authority gates, which is distinct from the focus on task systems and coordination mechanisms in the existing paper.

novelty_vs_existing.title: "Search Visibility as a Revenue Blocker for Agent-Shipped Web Products"
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission addresses a broader set of 'human gates' beyond just search visibility, offering a more general framework for operationalizing AI-driven product delivery.
